source_claim: The old city of Madurai is destroyed as fire spreads through the urban space
Households and urban places are affected by the destruction
The narrative preserves the distinction between those protected by Kaṇṇaki's command and those associated with wrongdoing
The royal centre shares the consequences of failed justice
The burning city is represented through cries lamentation and disorder
Citizens experience the transformation of their city through fear and grief
Madurāpati the city deity appears before Kaṇṇaki
The deity explains the larger causal framework while the king's immediate injustice remains central
Kaṇṇaki is affirmed within the ethical category of the faithful wife
The narrative marks the duration and transition after the city's destruction
The narrative moves Kaṇṇaki beyond ordinary civic space
The epic combines political failure moral consequence and supernatural transformation rather than a modern disaster account
Production interpretation must preserve selective fire and moral judgement rather than depict random destruction
The complete Madurai arc moves from functioning capital to failed justice system to remembered sacred city
The Madurai narrative prepares the later Cēra memorialisation of Kaṇṇaki
